steps.instruction: A scatter plot shows the relationship between two numerical variables. Each point represents one data pair.
steps.work: x-axis: hours studied. y-axis: test score. Each dot is one student.
steps.explanation: Scatter plots help visualize whether two variables are related.

steps.instruction: Look at the overall pattern of the dots.
steps.work: Positive: dots go up-right. Negative: dots go down-right. None: random scatter.
steps.explanation: The direction tells you whether variables increase or decrease together.

steps.instruction: How tightly do the dots follow a pattern?
steps.work: Tight cluster around a line = strong association. Wide scatter = weak association.

steps.instruction: An outlier is a point far away from the general pattern.
steps.work: If most points cluster between (1,10) and (8,40), a point at (5,80) would be an outlier.
steps.explanation: Outliers may represent errors, unusual cases, or different subgroups.

steps.instruction: Sometimes data forms distinct groups rather than one continuous trend.
steps.work: Example: a scatter plot might show two clusters — one group of young low-earners and one group of older high-earners.
steps.explanation: Clusters suggest the data may represent different subpopulations.

steps.instruction: Summarize: direction, form, strength, and any unusual features.
steps.work: 'There is a strong, positive, linear association between study time and test score, with one outlier at (2, 95).'
steps.explanation: A complete description mentions direction, form, strength, and outliers.
